Project 01

Site Analysis

Coffee Bike Site Analysis — Texas State University
San Marcos, Texas · Spatial Location Analysis
ArcGIS ProLocation AnalysisCartography
Spatial analysis for identifying optimal sites for mobile coffee bike stations on the Texas State University campus. Using pedestrian traffic density, building footprints, sidewalk networks, and roadway layers, the map visualizes foot traffic ranges from 169 to over 7,000 — highlighting high-density zones suited for placement. Lambert Conformal Conic projection applied for accurate local spatial representation.
Project 02

Demographic Analysis

San Marcos Demographic Analysis — Identifying Underserved Communities
New York & Austin Reference Areas · U.S. Census Bureau Data
PythonCensus APIArcGISDemographics
Multi-variable demographic study using the U.S. Census Bureau API, visualized through choropleth and proportional symbol maps. Explores socioeconomic indicators across census tracts — income, vehicle access, housing age and ownership, age distribution, educational attainment, and ethnicity — to identify spatially underserved communities. Project 03

Health GIS & Accessibility Analysis

PrEP Clinic Accessibility & HIV Vulnerability — Atlanta Metro
City of Atlanta, Georgia · Public Transit · Two-Step Floating Catchment Area (2SFCA)
Python2SFCAHealth GISBivariate Mapping
Spatial accessibility assessment for PrEP clinics via public transit across Atlanta, identifying communities with high HIV vulnerability and low healthcare access. Applied 2SFCA at 30-, 60-, and 90-minute catchments plus Gaussian decay. An HIV Vulnerability Index (0–1) was constructed from socioeconomic indicators and overlaid in bivariate maps against HIV rates and poverty.
Vulnerability Index
HIV Vulnerability Index (score 0–1)
2SFCA Models & Bivariate Maps

2SFCA — 60 min
2SFCA — 90 min

Project 04

Land Cover Change Detection

Land Cover Change Detection — Travis County (2010–2020)
Travis County, Texas · Remote Sensing · Flood & Urban Expansion Analysis
ArcGIS ProRemote SensingChange Detection
Remote sensing-based change detection across Travis County over a 10-year period. Land cover classified into 5 categories with a 25-class transition matrix tracking all type changes. The Unchanged vs. New Development map highlights zones of urbanization, vegetation loss, and flooding-related land shifts — directly relevant to flood risk and sustainability planning in rapidly growing Central Texas.
Change Classification Maps
Land Cover Change (25-class matrix)
Unchanged vs. New Development
Project 05

Urban Neighborhood Cluster Analysis

Neighborhood Typology & COVID-19 Vulnerability — Phoenix, Arizona
Phoenix, Arizona · GeoDa · Geodemographic Clustering · Sociospatial Inequality
GeoDaCluster AnalysisLeafletUrban Planning
Geodemographic cluster analysis classifying Phoenix into four neighborhood typologies based on socioeconomic, demographic, and housing characteristics. An interactive Leaflet dashboard enables side-by-side exploration of COVID-19 case counts and cluster classifications. Each cluster below pairs the spatial dashboard view with its neighborhood profile.
Cluster Profiles & Dashboard Views
click to enlarge
C0
Older, Predominantly White Homeowner Neighborhoods

Stable, long-term homeowners in northern Phoenix (Paradise Valley, North Scottsdale). Older population, low mobility, single-family housing. The citywide choropleth shows C0 areas experienced moderate vulnerability — buffered by housing stability and income despite age risk.

SuburbanOwner-OccupiedLow MobilityModerate Risk
C1
Affluent Professional Neighborhoods — 35.3% of tracts

High-income professionals in central/northern Phoenix (Camelback East, Ahwatukee). Newest housing, strong healthcare access, remote work capacity. Spatially clustered in north and central corridor with the lowest COVID-19 infection rates citywide.

High IncomeRemote Work35.3% of TractsLow Vulnerability
click to enlarge
click to enlarge
C2
Low-Income Diverse Immigrant Communities — 21.8%

Western and southern Phoenix (Maryvale, South Mountain Village). High immigrant/minority population, crowded renter housing, essential in-person workers. Spatially overlapping the darkest COVID-19 hotspots — the most vulnerable group in the analysis.

Essential WorkersRenter-Occupied21.8% of TractsHighest Risk
C3
Urban High-Density Apartment Areas — 27.1%

Downtown Phoenix (Encanto Village, Central City). Young mobile renters in multiunit buildings, heavy transit dependence, high turnover. Concentrated in the urban core where density and mobility amplified COVID-19 exposure significantly.

High DensityPublic Transit27.1% of TractsHigh Exposure
click to enlarge

Key Findings

Structural socioeconomic factors — income, occupation type, and housing density — were the primary drivers of uneven COVID-19 outcomes across Phoenix. C2 and C3 neighborhoods in Maryvale and Downtown emerged as priority areas for public health resource allocation.

Project 06

Urban Walkability Analysis

Kathmandu Valley Urban Walkability Comparison
Kathmandu · Bhaktapur · Lalitpur, Nepal · OSMnx · Network Analysis
PythonOSMnxNetwork AnalysisFolium
A composite walkability analysis of the three major cities of the Kathmandu Valley — Kathmandu (mean score: 0.44), Bhaktapur (0.28), and Lalitpur (0.19) — computed across 38,000+ street network nodes using OpenStreetMap data via OSMnx. The walkability score integrates proximity to amenities including supermarkets, schools, hospitals, pharmacies, parks, and bus stops. Results are visualized through street-level choropleth maps, a radar chart of amenity access by city, a score distribution histogram, and a walkability tier breakdown — together revealing that Kathmandu's historic core significantly outperforms its neighboring cities. An interactive Folium map enables node-level exploration.
Composite Walkability Map & Charts
Click to enlarge · Walkability scores, radar chart, distribution, and tier breakdown
Interactive Walkability Map — Kathmandu Valley (Folium / Leaflet)

Key Findings

Kathmandu's historic urban core achieves the highest walkability (mean 0.44), with 3% of nodes reaching "Excellent" (>0.70) — driven by dense mixed-use amenity clusters around Thamel, Durbar Square, and Pashupatinath. Bhaktapur (0.28) and Lalitpur (0.19) show significantly lower scores, with 68% and over 80% of nodes respectively falling in the "Poor" tier (<0.30), indicating a critical gap in pedestrian infrastructure and amenity access across the wider valley.

Project 07

Remote Healthcare Accessibility

Healthcare Facility Accessibility — Jumla District, Nepal
Jumla District, Karnali Province · Tobler Hiking Function · Terrain-Adjusted Travel Time
PythonTobler HikingEuclidean BufferVoronoiFolium
This project assesses spatial accessibility to healthcare facilities in Jumla District — a remote mountainous district in Nepal's Karnali Province — where terrain dramatically reduces real-world access compared to Euclidean distance assumptions. The study maps 32 facilities (21 Sub-Health Posts, 8 Health Posts, 2 PHCCs, 1 hospital) and models access using the Tobler Hiking Function adjusted for trail conditions (×1.4 trail factor). Results reveal that most municipalities face trail-adjusted walking times of 2–6+ hours to the Karnali Academy of Health Sciences (KAHS), the district's primary referral hospital. Population coverage is compared between centroid-proxy and Voronoi apportionment methods, showing the centroid method drastically overestimates coverage. All results are visualized in an interactive Folium map.
Methodology — Tobler Hiking Function & Population Coverage
Tobler Hiking Function & 60-min Walkshed by Slope
Population Coverage — Centroid vs. Voronoi Methods
Municipality Analysis & Facility Buffer Maps
Facility Load & Trail-Adjusted Travel Time by Municipality
Healthcare Facility Euclidean Buffer Zones (1/2/5 km)
Interactive Healthcare Accessibility Map — Jumla District (Folium / Leaflet)

Key Findings

Hima (366 min), Sinja (262 min), and Guthichaur (242 min) municipalities face critical trail-adjusted walking times to KAHS — far exceeding the 6-hour emergency threshold. The centroid method overestimates served population by up to 13× at 1 km buffers (92,709 vs. 6,918 by Voronoi), underscoring the danger of flat-earth assumptions in mountainous terrain. Only Chandannath municipality (25 min) achieves acceptable access, serving the largest population concentration of 28,420.

Project 08

Transit Catchment & Network Analysis

Melbourne Metro Transit Buffer & Network Walkshed Analysis
Melbourne, Australia · 20-Minute Neighbourhood · Network vs. Euclidean Comparison
PythonOSMnxNetwork AnalysisFoliumUrban Planning
This project analyzes walking catchment zones around Melbourne metro rail stations, comparing Euclidean buffer assumptions against true network walkshed polygons computed via OSMnx. The analysis covers 800m and 400m walking distances across five metro lines (City Loop, Upfield, Glen Waverley, Sandringham, Werribee) to evaluate the 20-Minute Neighbourhood planning concept. Network walksheds are consistently 65–80% of Euclidean buffer areas, demonstrating that road barriers, waterways, and discontinuous paths significantly reduce true pedestrian access. Results are visualized in a multi-station buffer map, a network vs. Euclidean comparison, and an interactive Folium map.
Network vs. Euclidean Comparison & Transit Buffer Map
Network Walkshed vs. Euclidean Buffer (800m · 5 stations)
800m & 400m Walking Catchment Zones — All Metro Lines
Interactive Transit Buffer Map — Melbourne Metro (Folium / Leaflet)

Key Findings

Network walksheds are consistently 65–80% of Euclidean buffer areas across all five stations analyzed, demonstrating that road barriers, waterways, and urban block structures substantially reduce true pedestrian access. The Footscray station shows the largest discrepancy, with its network walkshed fragmented by rail lines and river barriers. These findings directly challenge the use of circular buffer assumptions in transit-oriented development and 20-Minute Neighbourhood planning policy.
